narrative: As an Asian male applicant, your law school application was reviewed by an admissions model trained on data from thousands of students who went on to take the bar exam, looking at their academic records, test scores, and backgrounds to predict first-time pass rates. The model estimated a 45% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first attempt, which crossed the threshold for rejection since the school prioritizes applicants likely to succeed and pass that critical hurdle. This prediction stemmed from a close look at your profile, including your undergraduate GPA of 2.3—below the typical 3.21 average—your LSAT score of 23, far under the dataset's 36.7 average, your middle-family-income bracket, full-time student status, and school deciles of 5th for both first- and third-year grades, which sit a bit below the group averages of around 5.5 to 5.7. Among all these factors, your third-year grade decile stood out as the single strongest positive influence, pushing the model's view toward expecting you to pass because it showed solid mid-pack performance relative to your peers. However, your LSAT score had the next biggest impact, strongly pulling the prediction toward failure since it was notably lower than what the model sees in students who typically succeed. Your undergraduate GPA followed as the third key driver, also nudging the odds toward failure given its below-average standing compared to the broader student pool. While your full-time status aligns with most applicants and didn't sway things much, these top three elements—especially the LSAT—tipped the balance against you in this competitive process. It's tough to hear, especially when you've put in the effort through your grades and chosen full-time study like 93% of others. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost that score closer to the average, as it carries heavy weight here, and perhaps strengthen your GPA through post-bac courses or highlighting any upward trends in later deciles. Building a stronger narrative around your middle-income background and Asian heritage through essays or recommendations could also help in reapplying or appealing. Many students in similar spots have succeeded by targeting schools with more holistic reviews or improving one key metric—your path isn't closed, just needs targeted refinement. Keep pushing; resilience like yours often leads to bar passage down the line.
